coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS.

coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Xiaoli
Zhang, Congyang
Whitworth, Zackry
Zhan, Ziying
Chu, Kenneth
Hu, Ping
Jahanghiri, Sara
Zhou, Jigang
Chen, Jinxing
Zhang, Qiao
Ding, Zhifeng
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are nanocomposites that can be synthesized by the hydrothermal method. In this work, N-CQDs with the size of 3.2 ± 1.7 nm was prepared from 1 g citric acid and 2 g urea precursor in 10 mL water. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of the prepared N-CQDs with K2S2O8 as a coreactant was found to reach a high ECL efficiency up to 109% relative to that of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/K2S2O8, coreactant system, revealing their great potential for electroanalysis. It is probably because that N elements were doped well in this N-CQDs and increased presence of surface states per mass of N-CQDs. From the spooling ECL spectroscopy, it can be found that the ECL spectra exhibited both a red shift compared with their photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and a wavelength shift during the potentiodynamic scan in the ECL evolution and devolution processes, due to various emissive excited states of N-CQDs leading to higher ECL efficiency. This work gives an insight into development of high ECL efficiency N-CQDs for bioanalytical and light emitting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Electrochemiluminescence (ECL)
Spooling ECL spectroscopy
Relative ECL efficiency
Persulfate coreactant

We also thank the Electronic Shop in the Department of Chemistry and ChemBioStores at Western for their excellent service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of the as-prepared N-CQDs. The inset shows the high-resolution TEM image of N-CQDs. (B) The particle size distribution histogram.
(A) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s and (C) N 1s of the N-CQDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of urea (green), citric acid (blue) and N-CQDs (black).

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs (1 g/L) with an inset of its Tauc plot showing a direct band gap (r = 1⁄2). (B) PL spectra of N-CQDs dispersed in Milli-Q water (1 g/L), λ ex = 320 nm, 365 nm and λ em = 440 nm. The insets show the images of the N-CQDs dispersed in Milli-Q water under illumination of daylight (left) and UV (365 nm, right).
DPV curves of bare GCE in the absence (dash black) and presence (solid red) of 2 mg/mL N-CQDs in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) solution with 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte.

CV and the corresponding ECL-voltage curves of N-CQDs modified GCE in the absence (A) and presence (B) of 50 mM K2S2O8 in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) solution with 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte. The CV scan rate was at 100 mV s−1. (C) The accumulated ECL spectra of the CV experiments for N-CQDs modified GCE (red line) and bare GCE (blue line) in the presence of 50 mM K2S2O8 in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) solution containing 0.1 M KCl.

Spooling ECL spectra acquired at a time interval of 1 s for a N-CQDs modified GCE in the presence of 50 mM K2S2O8 in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 100 mV/s during a potential scan cycle between 0.1 and −2.8 V. Insets (A–C) are 2D viewings of the spectra following the same color codes.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: X. Qin, C. Zhang and Z. Whitworth contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are nanocomposites that can be synthesized by the hydrothermal method. In this work, N-CQDs with the size of 3.2 ± 1.7 nm was prepared from 1 g citric acid and 2 g urea precursor in 10 mL water. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of the prepared N-CQDs with K2S2O8 as a coreactant was found to reach a high ECL efficiency up to 109% relative to that of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/K2S2O8, coreactant system, revealing their great potential for electroanalysis. It is probably because that N elements were doped well in this N-CQDs and increased presence of surface states per mass of N-CQDs. From the spooling ECL spectroscopy, it can be found that the ECL spectra exhibited both a red shift compared with their photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and a wavelength shift during the potentiodynamic scan in the ECL evolution and devolution processes, due to various emissive excited states of N-CQDs leading to higher ECL efficiency. This work gives an insight into development of high ECL efficiency N-CQDs for bioanalytical and light emitting applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is the light emission generated by the reactions of highly active radical species produced in the vicinity of the electrode [1–4]. ECL has become a powerful analytical tool in clinic diagnosis and biomarker detection owning to its broad linear range and high sensitivity [5,6]. However, the conventional ECL emitters such as luminol and Ru(bpy)3 2+ compounds have some limitations. For example, luminol generates a weak ECL response in neutral aqueous medium and Ru(bpy)3 2+ is expensive. In recent years, various advanced nanomaterials have been introduced to improve the analytical performance of ECL sensors, such as metal nanoparticles [7,8], quantum dots [9,10], metal organic-frameworks (MOFs) [11–13] and carbon nanomaterials [14–16]. Until now, development of high-efficiency, economic and environmental-friendly ECL emitters is still a popular area of research.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a kind of smart probe, have high luminescent quantum yields, simple syntheses, strong biocompatibility, and low levels of toxicity and pollution, which make them a great candidate for ECL bioanalytical applications [17–21]. As we have demonstrated, CQDs can be prepared by the precursor solution from citric acid and urea [22]. Because citric acid is an ideal precursor due to its efficient carbonization ability and oxygen functional groups (–OH, –COOH) contained in citric acid provide abundant sp2 clusters for the growth and synthesis of CQDs [23]. It allows the graphitic structures to be separated from each other and control the grain size while endowing excellent hydrophilic properties [24]. The nitrogen-containing groups act as surface ligands to establish new emissive traps for excitons and induce blue fluorescence [25]. Thus, urea can be used as another precursor to generating surface ligands for nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) due to its high nitrogen content (–NH2) to promote element doping and surface functionalization. In fact, N-CQDs can be considered as one type of high ECL efficiency nanomaterials [22]. However, it is still challenging to further improve the ECL efficiency of N-CQDs.

In this work, N-CQDs with a high ECL efficiency were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method using 1 g citric acid and 2 g urea in 10 mL ultrapure water as a precursor solution. The as-prepared N-CQDs with a size around 3.2 ± 1.7 nm have been demonstrated that multiple C-, N- and O-containing functional groups on their surface. The ECL performance of the obtained N-CQDs in the presence of K2S2O8 was investigated by means of ECL-voltage curves and spooling ECL spectroscopy, which displayed a relative ECL efficiency as high as 109% and a unique potential-dependent emission characteristics.

Citric acid (99%), urea (≥99.5%), K2S2O8 (99.99%), Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O (97%) and the calibration reference ferrocenemethanol (Fc, 97%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Canada. Carboxymethylchitosan (CS, (C10H19NO6) n , 99%) was purchased from Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Inc. (Dallas, TX). KCl (99%) was bought from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA). NaH2PO4·2H2O (≥99%) and anhydrous Na2HPO4 was purchased from Caledon Laboratory Chemicals (Georgetown, ON). Ultrapure water (Milli-Q, Millipore, 18.2 MΩ cm) was used for dialysis during purification of CQDs and to prepare solution for electrochemical or ECL experiments. The dialysis bags with molecular weight cutoff 1000 Da (Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) were also used and stored at 4 °C in ultrapure water.

The N-CQDs were synthesized by a modified bottom-up hydrothermal method that we published elsewhere [22]. 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, and then heated in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 160 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the resulting mixture was transferred into dialysis bags for purification. During dialysis in bulk ultrapure water, the water was constantly stirred to remove small molecules, and it was changed six times over an 8 h period. After dialysis, the products were removed from the bag and dried through a freezing dryer. 5 mg of N-CQDs powder was dispersed into 1 mL ultrapure water for preparing the N-CQDs ink. The rest of N-CQDs was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future usage.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were acquired through a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI, Hillsboro, USA) with an operating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed via a KRATOS AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer (KRATOS Analytical, Manchester, UK) with the X-ray source generated by Al Ka operated at 360 W. The electron binding energies were calibrated according to that of the carbon 1s orbital. Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to identify the functional groups present in the sample via an INVENIO R & Hyperion 1000 spectrometer (Bruker, Madison, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectrometry was used to detect the absorption of the samples in the spectral range of 200–1000 nm. The spectra were collected by a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Shanghai, China). In addition, the photoluminescence and free excitation spectra of the samples were collected in a 10 mm quartz cuvette via a HORIBA Jobin Yvon FL-TCSPC (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, Paris, France) with a side entrance slit and a side exit slit width of 5 nm. Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was determined by a HAMAMATSU C13534 spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Shizuoka, Japan). The ratio of the number of absorbed photons to the number of emitted photons is the absolute PLQY.

The particle size distribution fitting method is achieved by using the Gaussian function. The results were fitted through Origin plotting software. The Gaussian function is provided below.
f
x , μ , σ
=
1
σ
2 π
e
−
x − μ
2
2
σ 2
where the σ represents the standard deviation, μ represents the mean of the data set.

All the electrochemical and ECL measurements of GQDs were investigated using a three-electrode system in a specially designed electrochemical/ECL cell, which has a flat and transparent Pyrex window on the bottom to detect light from the vicinity of working electrode. A glassy carbon electrode (GCE, d = 3 mm) was employed as the working electrode while platinum coiled wires were utilized as the counter and quasi-reference electrodes. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans were carried out on a CHI 610A electrochemical analyzer (CHI instruments, Austin, TX) with a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl as the electrolyte. The PBS solution was purged with Argon gas for 5 min to remove the oxygen dissolved in the system. The N-CQDs-modified working electrode was fabricated by drop-casting 20 μL of 5 mg mL−1 N-CQDs ink into the surface of the cleaned GCE. After drying at room temperature, 5 μL of CS (0.2 mg mL−1 in water) was used to cover the N-CQDs layer, ensuring that the N-CQDs layer was tightly attached onto the GCE surface during the electrochemical and ECL measurements. After each experiment, the ferrocenemethanol (Fc) was used as the internal standard to calibrate the electrochemical potential window. The redox potential of Fc/Fc+ couple in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl was calculated as 0.250 V vs. saturated calomel electrode (SCE) [25].

A photomultiplier tube (PMT, R928, Hamamatsu, Japan) biased at −750 V with a high-voltage power supply was used to collect the generated ECL, and then a Keithley picoammeter (Keithley 6487, Cleveland, OH) was used to convert it into photovoltage. The converted photovoltage and the electrochemical potential and current signals from the CHI station were simultaneously sent to the computer through a data acquisition board (DAQ 6036E, National Instruments, Austin, TX) to record current−voltage and ECL−voltage curves by a homemade National Instruments LabVIEW program [1].

The ECL spectra were measured as reported elsewhere [26] by a spectrograph with a grating of 50 l/mm blazed at 600 nm (Acton 2300i, Teledyne Princeton Instruments, MA) coupled with a charge coupled device (CCD) camera (Model DU401- BR-DD-352, Andor Technology, UK) cooled at −65 °C. Before ECL measurement, a commercial mercury-argon light source (HG-1, Ocean Insight, Orlando, FL) was used for wavelength calibration. Both accumulated and spooling ECL spectra were collected during potential dynamic scans.

The relative ECL efficiency (Φ ECL) can be determined relative to a commercialized ECL luminophore (Ru(bpy)3 2+) by taking its efficiency as 100% under the same condition using equation (1) as below [1].
(1)
Φ ECL
= 100 % ×
∫ a b
ECL × d t
∫ a b
c u r r e n t × d t
X
/
∫ a b
ECL × d t
∫ a b
c u r r e n t × d t
ST
where X refers to the studied N-CQDs/K2S2O8 system while ST represents the standard Ru(bpy)3Cl2/K2S2O8 system. The integration of ECL intensity and current vs. time indicates the photons generated and charges injected in the system, respectively.

In order to investigate whether the N-CQDs were successfully prepared, TEM was used to investigate the morphology and particle size distribution. In Fig. 1 A, most CQDs exhibit spherical features with clear boundaries and uniform distribution. Observing individual N-CQD at a magnification of 105,0000 × reveals clear lattice stripes with an interplanar spacing of 0.23 nm. It coincides with the (1120) crystal plane in a pristine graphite structure, demonstrating the existence of N-CQDs [27–29]. The particle size statistics analysis of N-CQDs demonstrated that the size distribution mainly focused around 3.2 ± 1.7 nm (Fig. 1B).

XPS and FT-IR were used to analyze elemental components and surface function group information of N-CQDs. In XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 A), three prominent characteristic peaks at 531.24, 399.74, and 284.74 eV are presented, corresponding to the electron binding energies of O1s, N1s, and C1s orbitals, respectively. It proves that N-CQDs mainly comprise C, O, and N elements, with carbon being the main component accounting for 53.92%, while O and N account for 26.23% and 19.85%, respectively. Furthermore, it could depict the nitrogen element's successful doping in the N-CQDs. In the high-resolution C1s spectra, three distinctive peaks are observed at 284.8, 286.9, and 289.1 eV, which may correspond to the signals of C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, and O–C=O/N–C=O groups, respectively [23,30]. The characteristic peak at 284.8 eV shows the highest intensity but relatively low electron binding energy (Fig. 2B). It is most likely due to the excitation of electrons by photons in the sp2 hybridized orbital within the graphitic structure. Further, it corroborates the primary structure of the N-CQDs observed in the HRTEM images. The co-existence of O=C–N and C–N functional groups in the high-resolution N1s spectrum (399.7 eV) does not show a significant peak splitting due to their relatively close electron binding energies (Fig. 2C) [31–33]. The above information demonstrates that N-CQDs are rich in O-, C-, and N- containing groups, a typical nanocomposite, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs, providing excellent water dispersity.

The presence of oxygen and nitrogen-rich groups was further confirmed in the FTIR results. As shown in Fig. 2D, a broad absorption appears in the range of 3650 to 2850 cm−1. This is composed of the characteristic peaks of N–H, O–H, and C–H, respectively, which gives the evidence of extensive hydrogen bonding within the N-CQDs sample; demonstrating the presence of aforementioned functional groups on the surface of the N-CQDs. The N–C=O peaks in the range of 1680 to 1620 cm−1 and the O–C=O peaks in the range of 1750 to 1700 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational absorption of two signature functional groups in urea and citric acid, respectively [25]. The characteristic peak above 3500 cm−1 is an adsorbed hydroxyl group attached to the carbon dots surface with weak binding ability. In contrast, the second –OH peak from 3000 to 3200 cm−1 represents the vibrational peak of the –COOH functional group. The characteristic peaks in the range of 1500 and 1200 cm−1 near the fingerprint region are the coupling of O–H bond bending vibrations and C–O bond stretching vibrations from the citric acid that constitutes this particular structure. Various shreds of evidence prove that these groups are indeed derived from the precursors while contributing unique optical properties to N-CQDs.

The Tauc plot is shown in the inset of Fig. 3 A, converted from the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of water-dispersed N-CQDs (1 g/L) showcased in Fig. 3A. The relationship is described by (ahν)
r vs. hv, where a, h, and ν represent the absorption coefficient, Plank's constant and frequency, respectively. The r stands for the power factor, which was applied to fit the data set. The direct bandgap transformations (r = 2) were found to be the best fit for the dispersed N-CQDs sample. The results agree with the previous report on CQDs synthesized through the solvothermal method [34–36]. As direct bandgap did not incorporate phonon during the transition process, thus, it favors radiative recombination and enhances PL efficiency. The plot's linear extrapolations (tangent line against the plot) to the interception of the x-axis yield information on energy absorption for the N-CQDs. The energy bandgap (E g) of the N-CQDs was determined to be 2.94 eV, corresponding to the photon absorption to promote electrons from the valence band to conduction band [36]. The above information points to the successful preparation of N-CQDs [22].

N-CQDs were dispersed in ultrapure water with a concentration of 1 g/L for PL testing. In the emission spectrum, it is observed that the emission peak at 440 nm has the maximum intensity when the N-CQDs are excited (Fig. 3B). No significant shift in the peak position was observed after excitation, with light wavelength varying from 275 to 430 nm respectively. The excitation wavelength independency may indicate that a single excitation pathway is sufficient for the excitation of different energy bandgaps in the N-CQDs. The other plausible hypothesis is that only one bandgap excitation dominates the photoluminescence process. This is likely due to the fact that the average particle size of N-CQDs is only about 3 nm. Previous studies found that almost all sizes of N-CQDs have an sp2 carbon center of about 1–5 nm, which is responsible for the emission [37]. In this work, the size of the synthesized carbon quantum dots is smaller than the threshold, and the core state of N-CQDs luminescence may be dominated by a single emission pathway. The core state luminescence is mostly short-wavelength [38], which could contribute to this near-UV blue light emission. The tendency of N-CQDs with amino groups functionalized on the surface tends to generate blue light emission. Thus, it may lead to the excitation of both carbon center and nitrogen-containing surface ligands when excited with a wavelength from 280 to 400 nm. Aqueous N-CQDs solutions at a concentration of 1 g/L were tested for PLQY. The highest quantum yield was 0.7%, and the best excitation wavelength was around 350 nm. However, no clear trend exists that different excitation wavelengths affect the absolute PLQY of the N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and ECL studies of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CV and DPV were used to explore the electrochemistry of dispersed N-CQDs in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) solution with 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte, as displayed in Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material (SM) and Fig. 4 , respectively. In Fig. S1, two small peaks can be seen at 1.1 V and −0.9 V. DPV is a good tool to reveal the redox behavior because of suppression of the background current. Thus, DPV was used to explore the reactivity of electro-generated species towards the formation of ECL-active excited species. Fig. 4 illustrates the DPVs of N-CQDs in aqueous system. Compared with the featureless DPV in PBS, the evident electrochemical characteristics appeared for N-CQDs, revealing the peaks produced in cathodic and anodic scanning potential range ascribed to the reduction and oxidation of N-CQDs. During the cathodic potential scanning process, two irreversible reductions appeared at peak potentials of 0.05 and −0.90 V. The anodic scan displays only one oxidation reaction at a peak potential of 0.90 V.

To investigate the electrochemical behavior of an N-CQD film, GCE was modified by drop casting 20 μL of 5 mg/mL N-GQDs, followed by 5 μL of 0.2 mg/mL CS to prevent N-CQDs dispersing in the PBS solution, as displayed in Fig. S2 and Fig. 5 A. It can be seen that a small oxidation peak at 1.40 V and an obvious reduction at beginning about −1.70 V appear in N-CQDs modified GCE. The reduction and oxidation peaks do not match those in the N-CQD dispersion presumably owing to the overall film resistivity increase from CS at the solid/electrolyte interface [22]. Although N-CQD illustrates good electrochemistry behaviors, no obvious ECL signal can be observed in the annihilation pathway. This is probably because of the poor stability of the electro-generated radical species in the vicinity of GCE, such as N-CQDs cation radical (N-CQDs•+) and anion radical (N-CQDs•−), which may not be stable enough to meet and produce the N-CQDs∗ excited state, emitting light upon relaxation to the ground state.

Therefore, in order to enhance ECL intensity, K2S2O8 was added into the solution to act as a strong oxidizing coreactant. Fig. 5B shows the CV with the ECL-voltage curve of N-CQDs-modified GCE in the presence of 50 mM K2S2O8 in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl. S2O8 2− is electrochemically reduced after the potentials more negative than −0.50 V, then it loses SO4 2− to become SO4 •−, which can react with a N-CQD•− generated at −1.70 V to produce N-CQD∗ and then release energy in the form of light. The maximum ECL intensity of N-CQDs with 50 mM K2S2O8 reaches about 180 nA at −2.70 V, which is 10-fold higher than those of bare GCE (19 nA, Fig. S3A) and 4-fold higher than those of dispersed N-CQDs (50 nA, Fig. S3B) in the same conditions. Then, the ECL intensity decreased upon scanning the potential in the reverse direction because of the concentration depletion of K2S2O8 and produced N-CQD∗. The ECL efficiency of N-CQDs/K2S2O8 system was determined to be 109% relative to that of relative Ru(bpy)3 2+/K2S2O8 system, which is higher than that of CQD10-40 (2%–96%) reported previously by our group [22]. Especially, it is 4.7-fold enhanced when compared with the same system of N-CQD synthesized by 20 mL starting precursor solution (23%), probably because that N elements were doped well in this N-CQDs and increased presence of surface states per mass of N-CQDs. During the CV experiments, accumulated ECL spectra of N-CQDs and bare GCE were acquired. As shown in Fig. 5C (red line), the ECL peak wavelength is at 695 nm, which is dramatically redshifted relative to the corresponding PL emission. However, a negligible ECL signal from bare GCE was observed in the presence of 50 mM K2S2O8 at the same potentials (Fig. 5C (blue line)), and dispersed N-CQDs in the solution also showed a small ECL spectrum signal (Fig. S3C). These results indicate that the enhanced ECL of N-CQDs-modified GCE can be attributed to the N-CQD•− reaction with SO•− to produce N-CQD∗, then giving off light. Furthermore, accumulated ECL spectra prove that ECL comes from the surface states of N-CQDs, which is similar to ECL emission of N,S-doped graphene quantum dots prepared by one-pot pyrolysis of citric acid and L-cysteine [25].

Spooling ECL spectroscopy is a useful tool to witness the evolution and devolution of ECL while scanning potentials to provide more insights into its mechanism, which has been developed and demonstrated by our group [1,26]. Fig. 6 displays the spooling ECL spectra for N-CQDs with 50 mM K2S2O8, collecting during a scan in the applied potential range between 0.10 and −2.80 V at 100 mV/s and color coding into three parts according to the potential region: red (forward scan, −1.60 to −2.50 V, Fig. 6A), blue (from −2.60 to −2.80 V and then back to −2.60 V, Fig. 6B) and green (backward scan, −2.50 to −1.50 V, Fig. 6C). Interestingly, the peak wavelengths present an evident shift during the scan. The onset of ECL was observed near −1.70 V with a near-infrared (NIR) emission peak wavelength of 865 nm (Fig. 6A). At this potential the N-CQDs was reduced to the corresponding radical anion (N-CQDs•−) (−1.70 V, Fig. 5A). At a close potential (onset and peak potentials at −0.5 V and −2.0 V, Fig. 5B), K2S2O8 was also reduced to produce a strong oxidizing species (SO4 •−), which then react with the negatively charged N-CQDs•− by injecting a hole into the HOMO of N-CQDs•−, producing an excited state of N-CQDs∗, which relaxed to the ground state and light emission. The spectra are blue-shifted with the potential scanned to a more negative value, and the maximum emission is 690 nm at −2.50 V. With the continuing scan from −2.60 to −2.80 V and then back to −2.60 V (Fig. 6B), the ECL emission reached the maximum at −2.60 V and the maximum emission peaks between 675 and 642 nm in the visible region. Moving from −2.50 to −1.50 V, the maximum emission of the spectra are red-shifted back to 810 nm in the NIR region, while the ECL intensity decreased upon scanning the potential in the reverse direction because of the depletion and reverse conversion of the SO4 •− and N-CQDs species (Fig. 6C). The results of ECL evolution and devolution from spooling ECL spectroscopy are matched well with that of ECL-voltage curve in Fig. 5B.

Compared with PL and spooling ECL spectra, the ECL wavelengths for N-CQDs/K2S2O8 system were shifted during the whole potential scan. Firstly, the different wavelengths between ECL and PL is because that the multistep electrochemical reaction needs time to produce excited states during the ECL process, which allow a relaxation for the excited states to surface excited states, while the excitation during the PL process is much quicker than ECL process. That is the PL mainly occurs within the core, while the ECL is surface state-sensitive [1]. Secondly, the peak wavelength variation during the potential scan in the ECL process is caused by the multiple C-, N- and O-containing functional groups (–OH, –COOH and –C=O) on the surface of the obtained N-CQDs, producing various excited states with different energy levels and thus a series of emissive traps. Different scanning potentials can give different driving force and produce the different emissive traps of N-CQD∗, generating different emission wavelengths [25].

In summary, N-CQDs were prepared as nanocomposite from citric acid and urea using a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method with a size around 3.2 ± 1.7 nm. XPS and FT-IR characterization demonstrated that the multiple C-, N- and O-containing functional groups exist on the surface of obtained N-CQDs, leading to excitation wavelength-dependent blue PL property and the maximum excitation and emission locate at 320 and 440 nm, respectively. Tauc plot converted from UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs also demonstrated the successful preparation of blue light-emitting N-CQDs. Furthermore, the ECL performance of the as-prepared N-CQDs was investigated by means of ECL-voltage curves and ECL spectroscopy. N-CQDs/K2S2O8 coreactant system displayed a strong ECL performance and a relative ECL efficiency as high as 109%, which is 4.7-fold enhanced when compared with the same system of N-CQD synthesized by 20 mL starting precursor solution (23%). Spooling ECL spectra showed different ECL peak wavelengths due to the various surface states and different surface energy traps at the surface of N-CQDs, which were distinguished from PL wavelength because the PL emission came from the core, while the ECL emission was produced from the surface excited states. The shift in wavelength during potential scan may be probably attributed to the multiple C-, N- and O-containing functional groups on the surface of the N-CQDs, leading to various surface states and thus producing variable emission wavelengths during the scanning potentials. These results proved that the size and ECL performance of the N-CQDs was dependent on the concentration of the precursor solution under the same conditions. This work provides insight into the development of high ECL efficiency CQDs for light emitting and bioanalytical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, DG RGPIN-2013-201697, DG RGPIN-2018-06556, DG RGPIN-2023-05337, SPG STPGP-2016-493924 (Z.D.)), New Frontiers in Research Fund, NFRFR-2021-00272 (Z.D.), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004034 (X.Q.)), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (China) (2020JJ5226 (X.Q.)) and China Scholarship Council (CSC, 201908430010 (X.Q.)). We also thank the Electronic Shop in the Department of Chemistry and ChemBioStores at Western for their excellent service.
